# UC-22.14.7 · Time Synchronization and Clock Skew for Audit Timestamps

## Description

Surfaces hosts whose event `_time` diverges from indexer time beyond tolerance, evidencing AU-8 time stamp synchronization.

## Value

Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST 800-53 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes os. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.

## Implementation

(1) Standardize NTP/chrony on all forwarders; (2) tune thresholds per sourcetype (batch logs vs real-time); (3) alert Tier0 assets on breach; (4) attach remediation runbook for clock drift. Indexes required: `index=os`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Universal Forwarder, Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `WinEventLog:System` (W32Time), `linux:secure` / `ntp` logs, `_indextime` vs `_time` delta.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Standardize NTP/chrony on all forwarders; (2) tune thresholds per sourcetype (batch logs vs real-time); (3) alert Tier0 assets on breach; (4) attach remediation runbook for clock drift.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=os earliest=-24h
| eval skew_sec=abs(_indextime-_time)
| stats perc95(skew_sec) as p95_skew, max(skew_sec) as max_skew by host, sourcetype
| where p95_skew>120 OR max_skew>300
```

Understanding this SPL

**Time Synchronization and Clock Skew for Audit Timestamps (AU-8)** — Surfaces hosts whose event `_time` diverges from indexer time beyond tolerance, evidencing AU-8 time stamp synchronization.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:System` (W32Time), `linux:secure` / `ntp` logs, `_indextime` vs `_time` delta. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Universal Forwarder, Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: os.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=os, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **skew_sec** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, sourcetype** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where p95_skew>120 OR max_skew>300` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare a sample of the same events in the source system (domain controller, cloud tenant, or device) the security team would open first. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (worst skew by host), Single value (hosts breaching threshold), Map (optional geo context)

## SPL

```spl
index=os earliest=-24h
| eval skew_sec=abs(_indextime-_time)
| stats perc95(skew_sec) as p95_skew, max(skew_sec) as max_skew by host, sourcetype
| where p95_skew>120 OR max_skew>300
```

## Visualization

Table (worst skew by host), Single value (hosts breaching threshold), Map (optional geo context)
